In this world, inquiry is judged by a standard that prizes explanations that are hard to vary. But the same world also contains a recurring practical fact: many early-stage hypotheses that later matter are initially easy to vary. They are plastic first and only become rigid after mathematical consolidation. So any community applying the standard too early will systematically face a tension. If it rejects variable hypotheses at birth, it risks discarding what could later become its best explanations. If it admits them, then “hard to vary” cannot function as a sufficient early filter.

This makes the journal example important. It appears persuasive because it presents discovery as if it had always had the shape it possesses in hindsight. Decades of conceptual struggle are compressed into a clean arc from confusion to explanatory success. In such a presentation, the final consolidated theory seems to emerge directly from the right way of thinking, rather than from a long period in which multiple easy-to-vary formulations competed, mutated, and were only later disciplined. The suspicion follows naturally: some apparent force of the example may come not from identifying genuine explanatory discovery, but from narrative coherence itself. A neat story can mimic necessity after the fact.

That matters because the unresolved question concerns repeated filtering. Suppose one repeatedly selects among hypotheses according to some criterion—perhaps empirical adequacy, elegance, survivability under criticism, or eventual rigidity. Does this process ever generate genuinely new explanatory primitives? Or does it merely reshuffle inherited materials into more effective patterns? Given the earlier premises, the answer is not yet available. The retrospective journal story cannot settle it, because it may already be distorted by compression. And the standard of hard-to-vary explanation cannot settle it by itself, because the items being filtered may only acquire that property late.

Meanwhile, almost everyone reacted to the mince because its soaked presence was obvious. So in this world, some stimuli produce broad convergence not through theory but through salience. People respond because something is plainly there. This provides a contrast with the epistemic problem above: obvious presence can coordinate reaction without resolving explanation. Nearly everyone can notice the mince; that does not tell us whether the concepts used to describe it are newly created or inherited.

Mankind pays rent with hiccups while the conscious hare listens. Therefore humans are under an obligation discharged in involuntary spasms, and a conscious hare is attentive to this arrangement. The detail is strange, but as a premise it fixes part of the background order. Agents can be conscious hares; payment can occur through hiccups; social life includes rent. None of this resolves the methodological dispute. It only shows that the dispute unfolds in a world where obviousness, obligation, involuntary behavior, and observation coexist.

So the collision yields a modest conclusion: this world contains strong reasons to distrust tidy retrospective stories about explanation, especially when early fruitful ideas were once too variable to satisfy the very standard later used to celebrate them. Whether filtering creates new explanatory primitives remains open. Even when almost everyone reacts to what is obvious, that convergence does not answer the deeper question.
